# Questions in Livestream 7

Live Stream #7: Seven and the Ragged Tiger

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15</a> | Did Psusennes I get the silver for his coffin by reusing older royal material, or from trade? | He thinks it likely came through trade or mining supply rather than reuse; silver was precious, harder to work than gold, and its use in Third Intermediate Period royal burials signaled wealth. | Falk says the silver probably came through trade and newer mining supply rather than stripped older royal material, since silver was rare in the New Kingdom and became especially significant in Third Intermediate Period royal burials. He adds that silver coffins and masks should not be read as impoverished substitutes for gold; silver was harder for Egyptian craftsmen to work and its use displayed wealth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:02</a> | What is your opinion about the Ezekiel 29-30 prophecy about Nebuchadnezzar plundering Egypt and leaving it desolate? Is there archaeological evidence? | He says there is evidence consistent with Nebuchadnezzar reaching Elephantine and plundering sacred treasures, after which Egyptian sacred barks and treasures appear more diminished. | He connects the question to Nebuchadnezzar's pursuit of refugees after Gedaliah's assassination and says the Babylonian campaign reached at least Elephantine, where Judean refugees had settled. In his summary, the later reduction in sacred barks, chests, and treasures is evidence consistent with Egypt having been heavily plundered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:40</a> | Is the mytho-history interpretation of Genesis 1-11 plausible, as endorsed by Gordon Wenham, Richard Averbeck, and William Lane Craig? | He agrees it is plausible: Genesis has historical aspects but is also mythologized and analogized in a way that fits ancient Near Eastern context. | Falk says he knows the position represented by Wenham and Averbeck and finds it plausible because Genesis 1-11 can have a historical aspect while also being mythologized and analogized. He frames this as consistent with ancient Near Eastern context and as one reason he remains cautious about making firmer claims on Genesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:01</a> | What do you make of Graham Hancock-style ancient timelines for Egypt? | He strongly rejects them, using the Sphinx and Giza limestone erosion as examples; he argues wind erosion over thousands of years explains the evidence better than a 12,000-year-old chronology. | He rejects Hancock's much older Egyptian chronology and illustrates the point with erosion on the Sphinx and Menkaure's mortuary temple blocks. Falk argues that Giza limestone has hard and soft layers and that wind-blown sand can abrade it badly over about five thousand years, so the erosion does not require a 12,000-year-old Sphinx. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:56</a> | What are good references for ancient Egyptian cuisine? | He mentions an ancient Egyptian herbal as the source that comes to mind, but says no specific cuisine book immediately comes to mind. | The only concrete source he can immediately name is an ancient Egyptian herbal that discusses herbs and spices used in Egypt. He says specific cuisine books do not come to mind during the stream and leaves room for other viewers to suggest better titles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:57</a> | What is the earliest text for the Opening of the Mouth ritual in the context of statues and the dead? | He believes the earliest reference is in the Pyramid Texts, in the Sixth Dynasty, and explains the rite as enabling the deceased's spirit and offerings. | Falk identifies the Pyramid Texts of the Sixth Dynasty as the earliest reference he knows. He explains the rite as ceremonially opening the mouth so the soul could come and go from the mummy and so the deceased could receive food and drink offerings after death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=866" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:26</a> | If Thutmose III was the pharaoh of Joseph and had a temple of Amun at Karnak, why would a Hyksos king have a temple at Karnak? | He rejects the premise: Thutmose III was not Joseph's pharaoh, the Hyksos did not conquer Thebes, and the identification makes no sense to him. | He answers that a Hyksos king would not have had such a temple at Karnak and rejects the proposed Thutmose III identification outright. His reasons are that Thutmose III was a Theban ruler, the Hyksos never conquered Thebes, and placing Joseph under Thutmose III is not a workable scenario for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:03</a> | Did Egyptians have real magic as seen in the plague narrative? | As a historian he does not answer whether real magic exists, but says Egyptians practiced magic and sometimes used tricks or rigged statues; whether there was actual magical power is impossible for him to answer historically. | Falk separates the historical question from the religious question: historically, Egyptians practiced magic, but he will not judge whether real magic existed. He mentions rigged divine statues and possible sleight of hand, then notes that the plague narrative moves from simpler signs to acts beyond what Egyptian priests could perform. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=1134" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:54</a> | What is the earliest record of Canaanite languages that we have? | He points to an unclear Syrian discovery name in the transcript, around 2350-2400 BC, while noting the language is not yet fully translated and the identification remains cautious. | He says the earliest Canaanite or West Semitic evidence would come from a Syrian discovery rendered unclearly in the transcript, dated roughly 2350-2400 BC in the Early Bronze Age. Falk distinguishes this from Akkadian as the earliest Semitic language and cautions that the relevant texts are not yet fully translated or widely published. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=1242" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:42</a> | Can you explain the "submarine and helicopter" hieroglyphs? | He identifies them as a palimpsest or superimposed writing at Dendera: one inscription was carved over another, creating the visual effect. | Falk explains the Dendera images as a palimpsest, where one set of hieroglyphs was overwritten by another and the overlap creates the illusion of modern machines. He adds that Egyptian rulers sometimes wrote over predecessors' inscriptions, and that deeply carved inscriptions, such as some by Ramesses II, were meant to prevent easy replacement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=1393" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:13</a> | What is your opinion of the hypothesis that only the Levites experienced an exodus from Egypt? | He says Friedman's Levite exodus hypothesis depends on the Documentary Hypothesis, which he rejects, so he thinks the Levite exodus loses its basis and does not hold up. | Falk presents the Levite-only exodus as Richard Friedman's attempt to preserve the Documentary Hypothesis by isolating Egyptian connections in one source tradition. Because he rejects that source-critical framework, he says the supporting structure for the Levite exodus collapses and the proposal is not persuasive to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=1554" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:54</a> | Plato mentioned Egyptian references to Atlantis. Is there any truth or corroboration? | He says there is no solid corroboration; suggestions such as Santorini remain speculative, and at present Atlantis is only a story. | He says there is no good evidence confirming Atlantis and treats Plato's account as an uncorroborated story. Falk mentions suggestions that Santorini or other events could lie behind the tale, but says those remain speculative without solid confirmation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=1663" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:43</a> | Will we ever be able to know how Egyptians pronounced their language? | He says Coptic gives a fairly good but incomplete guide; consonants are often clearer than vowels, and some signs remain disputed. | Falk says Coptic lets scholars reconstruct pronunciation reasonably well in many cases, but not perfectly. He notes that consonants are usually easier to recover than vowels, some hieroglyphic signs remain disputed, and pronunciation also changed over Egypt's long history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=1846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:46</a> | What advice do you have for studying Egyptian through books or websites? | He recommends university study if possible, notes his own introductory videos, and names Collier, Hoch, and Gardiner's Egyptian Grammar as useful resources, though Gardiner is hard to obtain. | He says the best route is a university course if one is available, because Egyptian is difficult to learn entirely alone. For self-study he points to his introductory videos and recommends Collier, Hoch, and Gardiner, while warning that Gardiner's grammar can be hard to find. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2020" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:40</a> | What religious papyri or texts should I read beyond the Book of the Dead, Coffin Texts, Pyramid Texts, and Sinuhe? | He says there are many religious texts, including stelae, wisdom texts, Papyrus Westcar, Karnak temple inscriptions, Thutmose III's annals, and anthologies by Lichtheim or Simpson. | Falk broadens the category beyond funerary corpora and says Egyptian religious material appears in stelae, wisdom texts, temple inscriptions, royal annals, and literary collections. He recommends looking through anthologies such as Lichtheim or Simpson because they gather many translated primary texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:47</a> | What is the story behind the Jews at Elephantine, and how were they distinct from their brethren? | He says they were Israelite refugees from Nebuchadnezzar who became mercenaries in Egypt, maintained Yahweh worship but also adopted other practices, kept ties to Canaan, and survived as a colony until roughly the first century BC. | He describes Elephantine's Judean community as refugees who fled after Babylon's actions in Judah and eventually served as mercenaries in Egypt. Falk says they worshiped Yahweh and kept communication with Judea, but their Elephantine temple and religious practices also show compromise and local adaptation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2489" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:29</a> | Jericho had grain still in storage and findings that accord with the biblical account; is that true? | He says charred grain is present at the Middle Bronze destruction of Jericho, but charred grain is common at burned sites and is not by itself remarkable evidence. | Falk accepts that charred grain is found in the Middle Bronze destruction level at Jericho, but says that is not unusual for a burned city. He cautions that the grain alone is weak evidence because stored grain frequently survives as charred material when a site is destroyed by fire. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:08</a> | Have any of Champollion's works been translated into English? | He says many major works have been translated, though not all journals, and some are freely available as PDFs. | Falk says several of Champollion's major works are available in English translation and that some can be found freely online as PDFs. He qualifies this by saying not everything, especially journals or lesser material, has necessarily been translated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:28</a> | Have you seen the Masonic temple in Washington, DC, that looks like an Egyptian temple? | He has not seen it, though he notes Masonic use of Egyptian symbolism and recalls visiting DC for an Egyptology conference and a National Geographic queens of Egypt exhibit. | Falk says he has not seen the specific Masonic temple, but he is aware that Masonic groups use Egyptian-style symbolism. He briefly situates the answer in his own DC visit for an Egyptology conference and a National Geographic exhibit rather than making claims about the building itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:38</a> | In Ezekiel, is the mentioned Daniel the biblical Daniel or a figure from another ancient tale? | He does not answer firmly because he has not studied Daniel closely enough; he notes there is another figure called Daniel and says he needs more study. | He declines to give a firm answer, saying Daniel is an area where he needs more reading before speaking confidently. Falk notes that another ancient figure named Daniel exists, so the identification cannot be assumed without closer study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=2834" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:14</a> | Would Moses' mother living at Avaris with Israel mean the pharaoh who adopted Moses lived in the eastern Delta, and would that disqualify late 18th Dynasty pharaohs? | He says no; Moses' adoptive mother would have belonged to the Ramesside clan as regional governors before Ramesses I, and late 18th Dynasty residence patterns alone do not disqualify them, though other data may. | Falk says the premise does not disqualify late Eighteenth Dynasty settings by itself, because Moses' adoptive mother could be tied to the Ramesside family before that line became royal. He frames the Ramessides as regional governors in the eastern Delta before Ramesses I and says other evidence matters more than residence alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:04</a> | If you could have the guitar of your dreams, what would it be? | His tastes change, but he mentions a Les Paul, Telecaster, and especially a Squier five-string bass as appealing, while noting he tends toward budget guitars. | He says his answer changes over time and lists several possibilities, including a Les Paul, a Telecaster, and a five-string Squier bass that appeals to him. Falk emphasizes that he tends to like practical, budget-friendly instruments rather than only expensive dream guitars. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3143" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:23</a> | Is the new synchronism about Terah connected to Zimri-Lim? | Yes. He says he will do a full episode on it, has checked the Mari source texts, and thinks it may support a northern Mesopotamian Ur rather than Ur in Sumer. | Falk says the synchronism is connected with Zimri-Lim and Mari, and he has checked the source texts enough to think it deserves a dedicated episode. He suggests the data may point toward a northern Mesopotamian Ur in the Abraham material rather than the better-known Ur in Sumer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:38</a> | Who is the best captain of the Enterprise, and why is it Robert April? | He says he prefers Benjamin Sisko over the Enterprise captains and was more into Deep Space Nine than the Enterprise series. | Falk answers playfully by sidestepping the Enterprise framing and choosing Benjamin Sisko instead. He says he was more interested in Deep Space Nine than in the Enterprise-focused series. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:03</a> | What is your opinion on FARMS, the Mormon apologetic group at BYU, arguing that the Book of Mormon is an ancient document? | He rejects the claim, saying the Book of Mormon is not an ancient Near Eastern document and, even by its own claims, would be a New World document. | Falk says he does not regard the Book of Mormon as an ancient Near Eastern text and is not persuaded by FARMS-style arguments in that direction. He adds that, on its own narrative terms, the text would be about the New World rather than the ancient Near East he studies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:52</a> | How good are James Hoffmeier's yellow and green books on Israel in Egypt and Sinai, and do you disagree with them? | He says both are generally good; the yellow Israel in Egypt book is approachable but out of date and getting a second edition, while the green Sinai book is more technical and not for beginners. | Falk evaluates both Hoffmeier books positively but distinguishes their audiences. He says the yellow Israel in Egypt volume is the more approachable starting point despite being dated, while the green Sinai volume is denser and better for readers with more background. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:29</a> | How can you tell if a king was Hyksos or an Egyptian pharaoh? | He points to name elements such as Seth associations in Delta or Hyksos contexts, inclusion or absence in Egyptian king lists, and process-of-elimination evidence. | Falk says the identification often depends on indirect clues rather than one simple marker. He mentions names associated with Seth and Delta contexts, whether a ruler appears in Egyptian king lists, and process-of-elimination judgments from the historical setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=3941" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:41</a> | Are there accounts of ancient Egyptians finding fossils or dinosaurs and incorporating them into myths or religion? | He says not really for Egypt, which lacks large dinosaur-type fossil deposits, though small marine fossils occur in Giza limestone; he contrasts this with other regions and mentions fossil shark teeth found in an Israelite context. | Falk says Egypt does not really provide evidence for dinosaur-like fossils being worked into myth or religion, partly because the relevant large fossil deposits are not characteristic of Egypt. He notes small marine fossils in Giza limestone, contrasts Egypt with other fossil-rich regions, and mentions fossil shark teeth from an Israelite context as a separate example. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:14</a> | What would be good reading material for Joshua's campaign? | He recommends Richard Hess and the volume Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament, edited by Greer, Hilber, and Walton, as good starting points. | Falk recommends starting with Richard Hess and with Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament, edited by Greer, Hilber, and Walton. He says that volume provides a useful perspective on current research and is written from contributors he characterizes as generally not minimalist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:42</a> | Is Seven and the Ragged Tiger the name of a Duran Duran album? | Yes. He says it was their third album and notes his fondness for Duran Duran and 1980s music. | He confirms the title is a Duran Duran reference and says Seven and the Ragged Tiger was their third album. Falk adds that he likes Duran Duran and much 1980s music, though he will not play clips on stream because of demonetization risk. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:19</a> | Are there problems translating bytdwd as "House of David" in the Tel Dan Stele? | He sees no problem with the translation, says alternative etymologies strain the evidence, and emphasizes that the stele was found in situ, supporting authenticity. | Falk says the Tel Dan Stele's in situ excavation supports its authenticity and that the House of David translation makes good contextual sense. He rejects alternative readings such as more generic beloved-language proposals because, in his view, they produce translations that do not make sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:36</a> | What do you think of the idea that Tutankhamun's sisters ruled or co-ruled before or during his reign? | He is doubtful and does not find the evidence compelling, though he acknowledges Amarna chronology has ambiguities and says stronger evidence could change his view. | Falk says the question falls into controversial Amarna chronology and he has heard the proposal argued, but he is not convinced. He would reassess if stronger evidence appeared, such as useful texts or wine jar dockets, but currently doubts that the evidence warrants placing Tutankhamun's sisters as rulers or co-rulers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4687" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:07</a> | What about claims that the Tel Dan Stele says "kettle" rather than "House of David"? | He treats such readings as overstrained attempts to avoid the House of David reading. | Falk treats the kettle reading as an example of trying too hard to avoid the House of David interpretation. He says that when interpreters strain that hard against a reading, it signals problems with the alternative proposal rather than with the standard translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4762" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:22</a> | What do you make of the scholarly view that Daniel was written in the second century, at least in parts? | He is skeptical but says he is not yet prepared to address Daniel's authorship in detail and needs more reading before giving a full answer. | Falk's instinct is skeptical of a second-century date, and he mentions the Septuagint as one reason he doubts that dating. Still, he explicitly limits the answer and says he needs to study Daniel's authorship in detail before giving a full treatment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=4859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:59</a> | What do you think of the statement in Tom Holland's Dominion that Moses is never mentioned in Egyptian sources? | He calls it an argument from absence and says preservation problems, especially for royal and Delta records, mean lack of Egyptian mention cannot bear that conclusion. | Falk says the claim relies on absence of evidence, which is especially weak for Egyptian records because the surviving corpus is incomplete. He emphasizes the poor preservation of royal court documentation and Delta-related records, noting that much of what survives from Egypt is religious rather than administrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=5025" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:45</a> | Would you create a Discord server? | He says maybe, but he does not know Discord or its maintenance burden and asks viewers to explain how it would help. | Falk does not commit to creating a Discord server because he has not used Discord and does not know the maintenance burden. He asks viewers to explain what benefits it would provide before he decides. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=5130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:30</a> | Was Avaris ever called Goshen, or something similar, by extrabiblical sources, or was Goshen purely a Hebrew name? | He says Avaris itself was not called Goshen by Egyptians as far as we know; Goshen is a regional designation in Hebrew texts, though new evidence could always change that. | Falk says Goshen is a regional designation in the Hebrew texts rather than a city name, which makes it harder to trace in Egyptian sources. As far as currently known in the transcript, Avaris itself was not called Goshen by Egyptians, though he allows that a new papyrus could change the evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=5219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:59</a> | Could the plagues have been a principal cause of Egyptian domestic trouble in the late 13th and early 12th centuries? | He says it is possible but hard to prove; there was upheaval and weakening after Merneptah, but evidence is insufficient to say whether the plagues were a principal cause or an exacerbating factor. | Falk says the period after Ramesses II, especially around Merneptah and after, shows increasing trouble such as Libyan pressure, Sea Peoples activity, and weakening Egyptian hegemony. He allows that the plagues could have contributed or exacerbated existing problems, but says the evidence is not enough to identify them as the principal cause. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=5436" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:36</a> | A man offered me a piece of the Ark of the Covenant for $2 million. Should I buy it? | No; he jokes that the viewer may already be wearing a piece of it and recommends his book on the Ark's Egyptian context instead. | Falk answers humorously that the viewer should probably not buy it and jokes that they may already be wearing a piece of it. He then points to his book on the Ark of the Covenant's Egyptian context for a serious study of the subject. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=5526" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:06</a> | Was Akhenaten's religious reform a reaction to the Exodus? | He says no, because Akhenaten's reforms preceded the Exodus in his model, but he sees those reforms as part of the religious soil from which later Israelite monotheism emerged. | Falk says Akhenaten's reform was not a reaction to the Exodus because, in his chronology, the Asiatic population at Avaris remained there through Akhenaten and until Ramesses II. He instead sees Akhenaten's religious shift as part of the background that helped later Israelite religion move from pantheism, localism, and henotheism toward monotheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/99jbuPoy8_U?t=5638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:58</a> | Any chance of a response video to Digital Hammurabi's series on Israelite slavery? | Maybe. He has not watched the series yet and says it belongs in the "maybe" bucket depending on other factors. | Falk says a response video is possible but makes no promise. He has not yet watched the Digital Hammurabi series, so he places the idea in a maybe category pending his review and other priorities. |
